source_title: Databricks: From Data to Decisions - [Business Breakdowns, EP.238]

paragraphs.speaker: matt
paragraphs.name: Matt Russell
paragraphs.text: We're breaking down Databricks, a $130 billion private company. I think this is the large private company that might be least understood by the general public. Most people understand what Stripe, SpaceX, or OpenAI do. Databricks is a bit more of a mystery to those not close to the business.

paragraphs.speaker: alan
paragraphs.name: Alan Tu
paragraphs.text: The challenge with Databricks is they address so many different use cases—recommending movies, pricing strategy, fraud detection. I think we've all had the experience of getting a data file in Excel and you want to run an analysis, but the data isn't perfectly set up. You end up spending 80 to 90% of your time just unifying all the data into the same format so you can run a simple calculation. That pain point of getting data into a format that allows you to ask even a simple question is the idea of data processing. In the case of Databricks, think of that at a completely different scale with tons of different types of data sources.

paragraphs.speaker: matt
paragraphs.name: Matt Russell
paragraphs.text: Let's go back to the beginning. There are all these clichés about academics not being commercial, but here you have this really impressive story of evolution. Can you tell a bit of the founding story?

paragraphs.speaker: alan
paragraphs.name: Alan Tu
paragraphs.text: It's seven founders that came out of Berkeley. They were all working in what's called the AMP Lab around the 2009 time period. That was the beginning stages of the cloud. The group gained conviction around the idea of cloud and thought about what problems they should work on within cloud. They ended up around this idea that data is going to be a really big problem, data at scale. One of the co-founders was the creator of Apache Spark. They believed data was going to be important, and they thought about using open source as another key bet. There were really three major bets: cloud was going to be big, data was going to be big, and open source was going to be a good way to build a business.

paragraphs.speaker: matt
paragraphs.name: Matt Russell
paragraphs.text: On the open source point, there are different ways to approach it. Can you talk about the commercialization? Offering things for free is a great way to get people in, but turning it into a great business feels like such a meaningful point in time.

paragraphs.speaker: alan
paragraphs.name: Alan Tu
paragraphs.text: There are a lot of examples of successful open source projects, but not very many examples of successful businesses built on top of open source. You need to hit two home runs: develop an open source technology that gets mainstream adoption, and then build a business on top of it. The problem is the open source technology ends up becoming one of the business's main competitors because anyone can get the product for free. Because the Databricks team wasn't really aware of the precedents, they thought about things in a very first principles way. The answer is you need to create a better product that is worth paying for. They created a new implementation of Spark that was completely proprietary, that had a lot more performance and things that enterprises want—reliability, scalability. They were unabashed about the fact that if you want to use this new implementation, you would have to pay for it.

paragraphs.speaker: matt
paragraphs.name: Matt Russell
paragraphs.text: I did want to touch on Databricks, the meaning. What's the origin story to the name?

paragraphs.speaker: alan
paragraphs.name: Alan Tu
paragraphs.text: The analogous thing to do would have been to name the company Spark. There are a lot of benefits to that because Spark was a very well appreciated name. But the reason why they went with Databricks is because from day one, they always felt like it was going to be more than just Spark. They thought about it as there were going to be many, many bricks that could all be used to apply around this broader problem around data. Underneath the decision to name it Databricks as opposed to Spark is this reflection of long-termism of thinking about what is actually going to set us up to become much more over time.

paragraphs.speaker: matt
paragraphs.name: Matt Russell
paragraphs.text: They've evolved in terms of what they offer and they have many different things under the hood now. Can you talk about the evolution of what Databricks offers to customers?

paragraphs.speaker: alan
paragraphs.name: Alan Tu
paragraphs.text: Databricks today has truly reached that point of platform. Following the success of commercializing Spark, they recognized who they were serving: data engineers and data scientists. They came out with MLflow, another product that extended the value proposition. They then came out with Delta, which was a first step towards data warehouses. There was a pivotal moment a couple of years ago when Databricks started to show the ability to address traditional data warehouse workloads.
